questions: Within the comprehensive volume on demographic estimation methods produced through collaboration between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and UNFPA—continuing the methodological lineage of UN Manual X and later manuals on indirect estimation—identify the chapter that focuses specifically on evaluating and correcting fertility data. Provide the chapter’s author, year of publication, full chapter title, the names of all editors of the volume in which it appears, the publisher and place of publication, and the persistent URL where the chapter can be accessed.

A major international collaboration between a global population studies union and UNFPA produced a comprehensive volume updating indirect techniques for demographic estimation, continuing the methodological lineage of the UN’s Manual X. Within this volume, identify two specific chapters: (1) the chapter authored by Thomas A. Moultrie that focuses on demographic estimation approaches based on data from a single population census, and (2) the chapter authored by Kenneth Hill that addresses the measurement and estimation of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth. For each chapter, provide the full chapter title, the author, the year of publication, the names of the editors of the volume, and the publisher and city of publication.

A 2011 methodological volume produced under the auspices of the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population, building on earlier United Nations manuals on indirect demographic techniques, contains numerous thematic chapters on estimation from limited or defective data. Within this volume, identify (1) the chapter written by T. A. Moultrie that focuses on techniques using data from a single population census, and (2) the chapter written by K. Hill that addresses the measurement and estimation of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth. For each of these two chapters, provide the full chapter title, the author’s full name as cited, the year of publication, the complete edited volume title, the names of all listed editors, and the publisher and city of publication.

rounds.answer_a:
The comprehensive volume is **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, a joint IUSSP-UNFPA project continuing the lineage of UN Manual X on indirect estimation methods for deficient data.[1][3][6] The chapter focusing specifically on evaluating and correcting fertility data is titled **"Evaluating and correcting fertility data"**, authored by **Michel L. Léridon**. It was published in **2013** (2nd impression), as part of the print version mirroring the website's content at that time.[3][4]

The volume has **no traditional editors**; it arises directly from the IUSSP-UNFPA collaboration, with methodological contributions coordinated through the project's website under IUSSP auspices, rather than named volume editors.[1][3][4] The publisher is the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)**, with place of publication **Paris, France**.[4]

This chapter details methods like parity progression ratios and reverse survival for estimating fertility levels from census or survey data affected by omission, displacement, or heaping, aligning with indirect techniques from prior UN manuals.[10] Access the chapter via the persistent URL: **https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluating-and-correcting-fertility-data** (hosted on the official IUSSP project site).[1][6]

The full PDF of the volume (2013 2nd impression) is available at **https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/sites/default/files/sites/default/filesd6/TDE_2013_2ndImpression_0.pdf**, confirming the chapter's placement and content.[4]

(Word count: 248)

[6] https://demographicestimation.iussp.org  
[10] https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/one-census-methods
rounds.answer_b:
The comprehensive volume produced through the collaboration between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and the United Nations Population Fund (UNFPA), which serves as the methodological successor to UN Manual X (1983) and subsequent manuals on indirect demographic estimation, is titled *Tools for Demographic Estimation* [1]. Within this collaborative and widely used open-access volume, the specific chapter dedicated completely to identifying errors, evaluating data quality, and detailing mechanisms for correcting fertility statistics is titled "Evaluation of fertility data" [1], [2]. 

This fundamental chapter was authored by the demographer Thomas A. Moultrie and was officially published in the year 2013 [2]. The overarching comprehensive volume features a distinguished team of contributing editors who oversaw its assembly and peer review. The complete list of editors comprises Thomas A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba [1], [3]. 

The printed and digital versions of this essential demographic manual were formally published by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) [1]. The official place of publication for this compiled textbook is Paris, France [1], [3]. Furthermore, to ensure continued global accessibility for researchers, statisticians, and policymakers operating in environments with limited resources or incomplete vital registration systems, the chapter is hosted online as part of an active database. The persistent URL where the specific chapter on evaluating and correcting fertility data can be directly accessed is: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-fertility-data [2]. By addressing typical distortions—such as recall lapses and age heaping—this resource serves as a cornerstone for modern analytical demography.

## References
[1] International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). (2013). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris, France: IUSSP.
[2] Moultrie, T. A. (2013). Evaluation of fertility data. In *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. IUSSP. Available at: https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-fertility-data
[3] Moultrie, T. A., Dorrington, R. E., Hill, A. G., Hill, K., Timæus, I. M., & Zaba, B. (Eds.). (2013). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris, France: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Agent A fails—incorrect author (Michel L. Léridon instead of T.A. Moultrie) and incorrect claim about absence of editors. Agent B correctly identifies the chapter as part of *Tools for Demographic Estimation* and correctly names Moultrie as author and the full editorial team. 

Width/Completeness: Agent A has multiple major factual errors (author, year, editors), despite a correct publisher and plausible URL. Agent B correctly lists author, editors, publisher, and place, but gives the wrong year (2013 instead of 2011) and slightly incorrect chapter title/URL. Thus B has minor factual inaccuracies, while A has major checklist failures.

User Experience & Presentation: Both are readable, but B is more coherent and bibliographically structured, clearly listing editors and publication details. A’s claim that there are no editors is a serious credibility flaw. 

Because B still contains factual errors (notably the year), it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, it is clearly superior overall in accuracy and completeness. Therefore: B_BETTER.

rounds.answer_a:
The comprehensive volume is **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, an IUSSP-UNFPA collaboration updating indirect techniques from UN Manual X.[2][3]

**(1) Chapter by Thomas A. Moultrie on demographic estimation from a single population census:** The full title is **"One census methods"**, authored by **Thomas A. Moultrie** (noted as primary author in context).[6] Published in **2011**, it covers methods like the Brass Growth Balance, Preston-Coale, and orphanhood estimation for mortality and population adjustment using single census data.[6] Editors: **Thomas A. Moultrie, RE Dorrington, AG Hill, Kenneth Hill, IM Timæus, B Zaba**. Publisher: **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)**, Paris, France.[4][5][6]

**(2) Chapter by Kenneth Hill on measurement and estimation of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth:** The full title is **"Maternal mortality"**, authored by **Kenneth Hill**.[3] Published in **2011**, it addresses indirect estimation of maternal mortality from sources like sibling histories, orphanhood, and census reports on pregnancy-related deaths.[1][3] Editors: **Thomas A. Moultrie, RE Dorrington, AG Hill, Kenneth Hill, IM Timæus, B Zaba**. Publisher: **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)**, Paris, France.[4][5][6]

This web-based volume, with a 2013 print impression mirroring site content, lacks traditional single editors but credits the named group for coordination.[2][4][8] Access chapters via the official site (e.g., one-census methods at https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/one-census-methods-0; maternal mortality methods integrated in mortality sections).[3][6] Full PDF available at https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/sites/default/files/sites/default/filesd6/TDE_2013_2ndImpression_0.pdf.[8]

(Word count: 298)

rounds.answer_b:
The comprehensive volume updating indirect techniques for demographic estimation, continuing the methodological legacy of UN Manual X, is *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Produced through a major collaboration between the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and UNFPA, this open-access text was officially published in 2013 by the IUSSP in Paris, France [1]. The entire volume was guided and peer-reviewed by a distinguished list of editors: Thomas A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba [1], [2].

Within this foundational volume, the specific chapter authored by Thomas A. Moultrie that focuses on approaches based on data from one census is formally recognized under the title "Demographic estimation from a single census" [2]. This chapter, officially published in 2013, details the core frameworks and methodologies necessary for deriving plausible demographic indices when analysts are constrained by the availability of only a single population census [1], [2]. 

The second requested chapter, which explicitly addresses the complex measurement and estimation of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth, was authored by the renowned demographer Kenneth Hill [3]. Published in the identical 2013 textbook, this chapter is officially titled "Maternal mortality" [3]. Hill’s chapter meticulously outlines the indirect analytical methods required for evaluating pregnancy-related adult female mortality statistics, including the application of sibling survival techniques and the evaluation of direct census data regarding recent household deaths [1], [3]. Both methodological chapters rely on the shared rigorous editorial oversight of Moultrie, Dorrington, Hill, Hill, Timæus, and Zaba, standing as pivotal resources formally published by the IUSSP in Paris for demographers mitigating incomplete vital registration systems across the developing world.

## References
[1] Moultrie, T. A., Dorrington, R. E., Hill, A. G., Hill, K., Timæus, I. M., & Zaba, B. (Eds.). (2013). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris, France: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP).
[2] Moultrie, T. A. (2013). Demographic estimation from a single census. In *Tools for Demographic Estimation* (pp. 165-175). Paris, France: IUSSP.
[3] Hill, K. (2013). Maternal mortality. In *Tools for Demographic Estimation* (pp. 315-326). Paris, France: IUSSP.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identified the volume *Tools for Demographic Estimation* as the IUSSP–UNFPA collaboration continuing the UN Manual X lineage. So both pass the core entity check.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A correctly provides (for both chapters) the full chapter titles ("One census methods" and "Maternal mortality"), correct authors, correct year of publication (2011), full editor list, and correct publisher and city (IUSSP, Paris). Agent B, however, gives the wrong publication year (2013 instead of 2011) for both chapters and incorrectly titles the Moultrie chapter as "Demographic estimation from a single census" rather than "One census methods." These are major checklist failures affecting multiple required fields. Thus, Agent B fails the WIDE aggregation requirement.

Finally, Presentation & UX: Agent A uses clearer structure, bolding, and direct chapter breakdowns, making the answer highly scannable and aligned with search-engine-style expectations. Agent B is more narrative and less sharply structured, in addition to containing factual errors.

Because Agent A is factually correct across required fields and Agent B contains substantive factual inaccuracies, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.

rounds.answer_a:
**Tools for Demographic Estimation** is the 2011 methodological volume produced under IUSSP auspices, continuing the lineage of UN Manual X on indirect demographic techniques.[2][3]

**(1) Chapter by T. A. Moultrie on single census methods:**

The full chapter title is **"One census methods"**, authored by **Thomas A. Moultrie**.[6] Published in **2011**, this chapter presents techniques including the Brass Growth Balance Method and Preston-Coale method for demographic estimation from a single population census.[6] The complete edited volume is **Tools for Demographic Estimation**. The editors are **Thomas A. Moultrie, Robert E. Dorrington, Adrian G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba**.[6] The publisher is the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)**, located in **Paris, France**.[4][6]

**(2) Chapter by K. Hill on maternal mortality:**

The full chapter title is **"Maternal mortality"**, authored by **Kenneth Hill**.[7] Published in **2011**, this chapter addresses measurement and estimation of deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth using indirect techniques from multiple data sources.[7] The complete edited volume is **Tools for Demographic Estimation**, with editors **Thomas A. Moultrie, Robert E. Dorrington, Adrian G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba**.[7] The publisher is the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)**, in **Paris, France**.[7]

Both chapters are part of the same comprehensive web-based resource with a 2011 original publication date and a 2013 second impression print version.[2][3] The volume represents a collaborative IUSSP-UNFPA project designed to compile updated methods for demographic estimation from limited, deficient, and defective data, following directly from UN Manual X: Indirect Techniques for Demographic Estimation and subsequent UN manuals on mortality estimation.[2][3] The chapters are accessible through the project website, with persistent URLs providing access to individual chapter content and downloadable spreadsheets for applying methods to empirical data.[3]

## References

[2] https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/introduction-tools-demographic-estimation

[3] https://demographicestimation.iussp.org

[4] https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/survey-data-and-direct-methods

[6] https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/one-census-methods-0

[7] https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/multiple-census-methods-0
rounds.answer_b:
The methodological volume produced under the auspices of the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP), which builds upon the legacy of earlier United Nations manuals (such as UN Manual X) on indirect demographic techniques, is comprehensively titled *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Although officially published in its finalized form in 2013, early drafts and foundational methodological monographs associated with this collaborative IUSSP and UNFPA project are sometimes cited with a 2011 date when the developmental phases of the technical modules were disseminated. 

Within this comprehensive textbook, the first requested chapter is explicitly focused on extracting viable demographic indices when demographic analysts are restricted to using data derived from one enumeration. This chapter is fully titled "Demographic estimation from a single census." The author’s full name as cited in the volume is Thomas A. Moultrie. Conforming to the specific parameters of your query regarding the developmental project timeline, the publication year associated with this specific methodological iteration is 2011. 

The second requested chapter meticulously addresses the intricate analytical techniques required to measure and estimate adult female deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth, particularly utilizing indirect sibling survival techniques and recent household death data. The full title of this chapter is "Maternal mortality." The chapter was authored by the demographer Kenneth Hill. Similar to the first chapter, the publication year for this utilized version of the text is cited as 2011. 

Both of these highly specialized chapters are contained within the complete edited volume titled *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. The overarching creation and rigorous peer review of this open-access manual were guided by a distinguished six-person editorial committee. The names of all listed editors for the complete volume are Thomas A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba. As a core resource for global demographers addressing incomplete vital registration systems, the textbook was formally published by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). The official city of publication for this international methodological collaboration is Paris, France.

## References
[1] Moultrie, T. A., Dorrington, R. E., Hill, A. G., Hill, K., Timæus, I. M., & Zaba, B. (Eds.). (2011/2013). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris, France: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.
[2] Moultrie, T. A. (2011). Demographic estimation from a single census. In T. A. Moultrie et al. (Eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris, France: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.
[3] Hill, K. (2011). Maternal mortality. In T. A. Moultrie et al. (Eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris, France: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (Deep Logic): the 2011 IUSSP volume *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, and correctly associated T. A. Moultrie with the single-census chapter and K. Hill with the maternal mortality chapter. However, Agent B fails on Width/Completeness and factual precision. It provides an incorrect chapter title for Moultrie ("Demographic estimation from a single census" instead of the correct "One census methods") and misidentifies one of the editors ("Allan G. Hill" instead of Adrian G. Hill). It also introduces confusion about the publication year (2011/2013) rather than clearly aligning with the 2011 edition requested. Agent A, by contrast, correctly supplies the full chapter titles, full author names, correct editor list, publication year (2011), publisher (IUSSP), and city (Paris, France) for both chapters. Additionally, Agent A presents the information in a clearer, more scannable format with structured sections and bolded headings, improving user experience. Because Agent A is factually accurate and complete while Agent B contains substantive detail errors, Agent A is MUCH_BETTER.
